Title will be updated.

Ziqi Kang, Andreas Hainari, Venla Kaislo, Saundarya Shah, Lina
Maltrovsky, Anastasia Lundgren, Essi Kahelin, Matilda Salko, Anni
Virtanen, Ulla-Majia Haltia, Anniina Färkkila

# Abstract

# Introduction

High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most lethal subtype of
epithelial ovarian cancer, due to late-stage diagnosis, high rates of
recurrence, and frequent resistance to therapy. Based on the ability of
genomic recombination repair, HGSC tumors are commonly stratified into
two major subtypes: homologous recombination deficient (HRD) and
homologous recombination proficient (HRP). HRD tumors typically contain
BRCA mutations and are more responsive to platinum-based chemotherapy
and PARP inhibitors. In contrast, HRP tumors lack these deficiencies and
often show resistance to PARP inhibitor treatment. Emerging evidence
indicates that genomic instability and the resulting genomic damage may
shape the tumor microenvironment (TME), influencing spatial tissue
architecture and modulating immune cell behavior. Specifically, damaged
or stressed tumor cells may evade immune surveillance through altered
cytokine signaling, immune exclusion, or recruitment of
immunosuppressive cell populations.

**"Intertumoral TME heterogeneity established before therapeutic
interventions often portends diverse patient outcomes."** To investigate

t of
immunosuppressive cell populations.

**"Intertumoral TME heterogeneity established before therapeutic
interventions often portends diverse patient outcomes."** To investigate
how genomic background interacts with immune escape mechanisms, we
analyzed chemo-naive HGSC samples stratified by clinical
outcome---progression versus progression-free status. Within each
outcome group, we compared the TME features of HRD and HRP tumors. This
approach allowed us to assess how immune and spatial TME characteristics
differ between HRD and HRP tumors under similar clinical conditions. Our
goal was to identify subtype-specific immune escape strategies and
spatial TME patterns that may underlie differential patient outcomes and
provide a rationale for tailored therapeutic interventions.

\[Then more about CNA mutation and fine-grained patient subtypes -\>
genetic drivers?\]

We incorporate multi-omics profiling using whole exome sequencing (WES),
bulk RNA sequencing, high-dimensional whole-section tissue imaging
(t-CycIF), and spatial transcriptomics from 119 (80+39) patient samples
capturing over 58 million single cells and XX spatial transcriptomic
domains captured by molecular profiling. By combining genomic,
single-cell, and spatial data from patient-matched HGSC samples
collected from chemo-naive patients, we show how the spatially cell-cell

molecular profiling. By combining genomic,
single-cell, and spatial data from patient-matched HGSC samples
collected from chemo-naive patients, we show how the spatially cell-cell
interaction neighborhoods and transcriptional programs varies due to
genetic drivers, resulting....

# Result

## Single-cell spatial characterization reveals distinct TME states across HGSC subgroups

Content:

- Clinical cohort description (N, HRD/HRP, PFS distribution, tissue
 > sites, treatment patterns).

- Overview of data layers: WES, bulk RNA-seq, t-CyCIF, spatial
 > transcriptomics. ￼

- Clear definition of the discovery cohort vs second ADC cohort, and
 > which analyses are done in which.

Key analyses to add/clarify:

- Kaplan--Meier curves for HRD vs HRP in the discovery cohort
 > (baseline).

- Swimmer plots to show the HRD & HRP patients' OS and PFS?

- A simple Cox model with HRD/HRP + key clinical covariates as a
 > starting point.

This establishes HRD/HRP as a known but incomplete explanation of
outcome and motivates adding TME.

To investigate the effects of genetic drivers on the TME in the setting
of patients unaffected by therapy, we collected a series of 89
chemo-naive distinct tumor samples from 80 patients with high-grade
serous ovarian cancer (HGSC), enrolled in the Oncosys-OVA prospective
observational trial

lected a series of 89
chemo-naive distinct tumor samples from 80 patients with high-grade
serous ovarian cancer (HGSC), enrolled in the Oncosys-OVA prospective
observational trial
([[NCT06117384]{.underline}](https://clinicaltrials.gov/show/NCT06117384)).
All tumor tissue profiles were obtained from primary debulking surgery,
obtained mainly from metastatic omental sites (n = 62), otherwise from
the adnexa (ovaries/fallopian tube, n = 27). Detailed clinical
annotations, including molecular features (OvaHRDscore), treatments (for
example, PARP inhibitor) as well as surgical and survival outcomes were
collected (Supplementary Table X). The tumor samples underwent
multi-omics profiling with whole-exome sequencing (WES), bulk RNA-seq,
tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) imaging, and spatial
transcriptomics (Figure 1X). We obtained DNA sequencing data spanning a
xx-gene panel, focused on single-nucleotide variations, somatic CNAs,
chromosomal rearrangements, and tumor mutational burden (TMB), providing
a basis to link tissue structure and genetic aberrations. The WES
analysis results were summarised in integrative Molecular Tumor Board
(iMTB) reports.

To characterize the cellular landscape of these chemo-naive tumors, we
integrated data across platforms. We applied whole-slide t-CycIF imaging
to profile the spatial architecture of the single-cell TME, and

landscape of these chemo-naive tumors, we
integrated data across platforms. We applied whole-slide t-CycIF imaging
to profile the spatial architecture of the single-cell TME, and
successfully segmented and quantified 58,561,942 cells across 79 tumor
samples (Fig. SX). We phenotyped 10 cell types using open-source
packages Tribus and SCIMAP, including tumor cells, stromal cells,
cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAF), B cells, CD4⁺ T cells, CD8⁺ T
cells, regulatory T cells (Tregs), NK cells, dendritic cells (DCs), and
macrophages. In parallel, we inferred fine-grained cell type proportions
from bulk RNA-seq data using Kassandra, and inferred the expression
matrix of tumor cells using PRISM. Across matched tumor sites and
patients, estimated cell type proportions were consistent between
t-CyCIF and bulk RNA-seq. The proportion of tumor cells were consistent
with tumor purity estimation on WES data by ASCAT.

Initial analyses of the data reveal heterogeneous tumor tissue
compositions across patients. At the level of major cell types, we
observed statistically significant trends in line with previous studies
toward an increase of CAF and Treg, while a decrease of DCs and
macrophage cells in pre-treatment metastatic tumors. Similarly
consistent with previous studies, HRD samples have a significantly
higher proportion of CD8T cells compared with HRP samples, but have a

pre-treatment metastatic tumors. Similarly
consistent with previous studies, HRD samples have a significantly
higher proportion of CD8T cells compared with HRP samples, but have a
lower proportion of CD4T cells. These features, established before any
therapeutic intervention, may reflect inherent immunological differences
shaped by genomic background and underlie divergent clinical outcomes.
To explore this further, we compared HRD and HRP tumors within
clinically defined progression and progression-free groups. The same
trend in immune composition---higher CD8⁺ and lower CD4⁺ in HRD versus
HRP---was observed in the progression-free subgroup but was absent in
the progression subgroup. This suggests that tumors from patients who
progress may diverge from the canonical immune phenotypes of HRD and HRP
subtypes, resulting in a diminished difference in CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T cell
composition between the two groups. These atypical immune landscapes in
progressing tumors may reflect biological selection pressures or
alternative immune escape routes. To investigate structural features of
the immune microenvironment, we next performed spatial analyses of TME
organization.

- While compositional shifts suggest underlying differences, they
 > cannot further explain how immune cells are spatially organized
 > --- prompting spatial analyses of the TME.

## HR status and progression relate to distinct immune landscapes in the TME

**Tissue-scale spatial neighborhoods (RCNs) and genomic background**

(Currently: "HR status and progression relate to distinct immune
landscapes in the TME" and RCN analysis.)

Content:

- Derivation of 13 recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) using
 > SCIMAP.

- Description of key neighborhoods: tumor-only (RCN1), highly diverse
 > immune-rich (RCN13), stromal neighborhoods, and tumor--stroma
 > interface RCNs.

- HRD vs HRP enrichment of RCNs, with emphasis on RCN13 and TSI-like
 > RCNs.

Analyses to add/refine:

- Per-patient RCN enrichment scores, then model:

 - RCN13 (or other RCNs?) \~ HR status + progression + HR ×
 > progression.

- Cluster patients by RCN composition profile to obtain a first,
 > high-level set of "TME neighborhood patterns".

- Optional: associate RCN-based clusters with PFS by KM curves
 > (unadjusted), as an early indication that spatial composition
 > already relates to outcome.

This sets up the idea that spatial neighborhoods differ by genomic
subtype beyond simple cell fractions.

To systematically analyze the spatial distribution of cell communities

.

This sets up the idea that spatial neighborhoods differ by genomic
subtype beyond simple cell fractions.

To systematically analyze the spatial distribution of cell communities
within the HGSC TME, we annotated 13 distinct recurrent cellular
neighborhoods (RCNs) by clustering the cellular neighborhood matrix with
the SCIMAP package. The identified RCNs included one tumor
cell-dominated neighborhood (RCN1), three stromal neighborhoods (RCN2,
RCN8, RCN9), six immune-rich neighborhoods, and three neighborhoods
localized at the tumor--stroma interface (TSI)---RCN6, RCN10, and
RCN12---each containing mixed populations of tumor, stromal, and immune
cells. To quantify the cellular heterogeneity within each neighborhood,
we computed Rao's quadratic entropy based on cell type proportions.
RCN13 has the highest diversity (0.842) composed of tumor cells, stromal
cells, and nearly all major immune cell types, with particularly high
representation of CD4⁺ T cells. The TSI-related RCNs (RCN6, 10, and 12)
showed moderately high diversity scores (\~0.7), reflecting their mixed
cellular composition. In contrast, RCN1---dominated by tumor cells---had
the lowest diversity score (0.108), consistent with a spatially
homogeneous, immune-excluded tumor niche.

We then explored whether the RCNs based on spatial TME neighborhood
compositions was driven by genomic backgrounds.

tent with a spatially
homogeneous, immune-excluded tumor niche.

We then explored whether the RCNs based on spatial TME neighborhood
compositions was driven by genomic backgrounds. Comparing the enrichment
of RCNs across HRD and HRP groups, we found that RCN13, RCN3 and RCN9
are significantly enriched in HRP groups with RCN13 showing the largest
log₂ fold change. RCN13's high cellular diversity---marked by broad
immune infiltration and abundant CD4⁺ T cells---suggests that HRP tumors
more frequently support immunologically complex spatial niches. RCN3 and
RCN9 represent the stroma-resident immune cell and CAF-enriched
stromal--tumor interfaces. Together, the results highlight that the
spatial architecture of the tumor microenvironment also differs between
HRD and HRP tumors.

While RCN analysis captures spatial patterns that are consistent across
tissues, it does not resolve patient-specific or fine-grained spatial
features. To address this, we applied spatial point-level analysis using
our in-house package, SPACEstat, focusing on immune infiltration
patterns and the spatial positioning of immune cells relative to the
tumor--stroma interface (TSI) in individual HGSC samples. Specifically,
we quantified the distribution of cell distances to the TSI and
calculated an immune infiltration index, defined as the ratio of immune

) in individual HGSC samples. Specifically,
we quantified the distribution of cell distances to the TSI and
calculated an immune infiltration index, defined as the ratio of immune
cell density in tumor regions to that in stromal regions (see Methods).
Comparing HRD and HRP tumors, we found that HRD samples showed
significantly lower densities of CD4⁺ T cells in both tumor and stromal
compartments, but higher densities of dendritic cells (DCs) and CD8⁺ T
cells within tumor regions. In terms of spatial positioning, HRP tumors
tended to show higher immune cell proximity to the TSI across immune
cell types, although these differences did not reach statistical
significance.

**Tumor--stroma interface (TSI) positioning and immune infiltration
patterns**

(Currently the SPACEstat TSI proximity and infiltration index analyses.)

- Definitions: TSI, immune distances to TSI, immune infiltration index
 > (tumor vs stroma densities).

- Main findings:

 - Contrasting HRD vs HRP in terms of TSI proximity and
 > infiltration, in progression vs non-progression groups.

 - The observation that in HRP, immune proximity to TSI stratifies
 > progression, whereas in HRD it does not.

 > patient (e.g. "TSI_proximity_CD8", "TSI_proximity_B",
 > "TSI_exclusion_score").

 > "TSI_exclusion_score").

 - PFS \~ TSI score + Clin (within HRD)

This section formalizes that TSI metrics are prognostic in HRP but not
HRD, which is a key message you already hint at in text.

We then performed subgroup comparisons based on clinical progression
status. Among progression samples, B cells, CD4⁺ T cells, and CD8⁺ T
cells were significantly closer to the TSI in HRD tumors compared to HRP
tumors, both when measured globally and from the stromal side. Within
tumor regions, only B cells showed significantly higher proximity to the
TSI in HRD tumors. B cells also had a higher immune infiltration index
in HRD samples, despite exhibiting lower stromal densities. CD8⁺ T cells
similarly showed higher infiltration indices in HRD tumors. In the
progression-free subgroup, the trends were generally reversed. Most
immune cell types---except NK cells---exhibited greater TSI proximity in
HRP tumors compared to HRD tumors. B cells in HRD tumors also had lower

ends were generally reversed. Most
immune cell types---except NK cells---exhibited greater TSI proximity in
HRP tumors compared to HRD tumors. B cells in HRD tumors also had lower
infiltration indices and higher stromal densities relative to HRP
tumors. For CD4⁺ T cells, both tumor and stromal densities, as well as
the infiltration index, were significantly lower in HRD tumors. CD8⁺ T
cells, however, showed higher densities in both tumor and stroma in HRD
tumors, consistent with earlier observations based on cellular
proportions. These comparisons indicate that the spatial distribution of
immune cells relative to the tumor--stroma interface differs between HRD
and HRP tumors, and that these differences vary between progression and
non-progression groups.

To further assess whether these spatial features distinguish clinical
outcomes within each genomic background, we next compared spatial
metrics between progression and non-progression samples separately in
HRD and HRP groups. We examined immune spatial metrics within each
genomic background to evaluate associations with clinical outcome. Among
HRP tumors, non-progression samples exhibited significantly higher TSI
proximity across all immune cell types, along with higher B cell
infiltration indices and increased CD4⁺ T cell density in the tumor
region. By contrast, HRD tumors showed no statistically significant

immune cell types, along with higher B cell
infiltration indices and increased CD4⁺ T cell density in the tumor
region. By contrast, HRD tumors showed no statistically significant
differences in immune proximity, infiltration index, or immune cell
densities between progression and non-progression groups, with the
exception of a slight reduction in B cell infiltration index in
non-progression samples. These findings indicate that HRD and HRP tumors
exhibit different patterns of immune spatial organization in the
treatment-naive setting, with greater variation across clinical
progression groups in HRP tumors. The relatively uniform features
observed in HRD tumors may indicate that additional spatial dimensions
beyond TSI proximity and infiltration are needed to capture biologically
relevant heterogeneity. We next examined higher-order spatial structures
to further dissect immune cell organization in the treatment-naive TME.

- Immune positioning relative to the TSI further stratified outcomes
 > in HRP but not HRD tumors. These results highlight the importance
 > of higher-order spatial architecture, motivating us to move beyond
 > neighborhoods and test whether coordinated immune structures
 > underlie these subtype-specific patterns.

##

## [Interaction of lymphonet and myelonet as key factor of HRP prognosis]{.mark} 

\[immune network analysis!\]

Content:

- Construction of lymphoid and myeloid networks via Delaunay
 > triangulation and definition of immune hubs (overlapping
 > components).

- Quantification of hub count, hub size, cellular composition, and
 > spatial clustering (Ripley's L, g(r)).

- Patient-level clustering into hub-architecture classes.

Analyses to add/refine:

- Derive one or two summary "hub architecture scores": e.g. hub
 > density (events per mm²), degree of clustering, lymphoid:myeloid
 > balance.

- Within HRP:

 - KM curves comparing patients with low vs high hub density.

 - Cox: PFS \~ hub density + Clin \[+/- TSI scores\].

 - Show that -\> (can I?) immune hub scarcity remains associated
 > with poor prognosis in HRP even after adjusting for baseline
 > composition and TSI metrics, thereby positioning hubs as a
 > higher-order architectural feature.

To profile higher-order coordination among immune lineages, we modeled
spatial networks of lymphoid (B, CD4⁺, CD8⁺, NK, Treg) and myeloid
(macrophage, DC) cells across whole-slide images. Overlapping lymphoid
and myeloid network components formed immune interaction
events---referred to as immune hubs---representing locally coordinated
immune aggregates.

de images. Overlapping lymphoid
and myeloid network components formed immune interaction
events---referred to as immune hubs---representing locally coordinated
immune aggregates.

Across all samples, immune hubs varied substantially in size, cellular
composition, and spatial density. We defined immune interaction events
("immune hubs") by overlapping lymphoid (B, CD4⁺, CD8⁺, NK, Treg) and
myeloid (DC, macrophage) network components (edge ≤35 µm,
inter-component distance \<10 µm, ≥30 cells), then summarized each hub's
size, composition, and centroid location. Using hub centroids, we
applied ecological point-pattern statistics (Ripley's L(r) and the
pair-correlation function g(r)) and derived curve features (e.g.,
max_l_obs, r_maxL, r_decayL, slope_at_origin, max_pcf, r_maxg, r_decayg,
mean_pcf_short, mean_pcf_mid) to cluster patients by spatial hub
organization. N (8) clusters were generated in total.

Preliminary analyses revealed that a subgroup of HRP patients exhibited
very few immune interaction events, suggesting sparse lymphoid--myeloid
coordination. Notably, these HRP tumors also tended to belong to the
progression group, hinting that disrupted immune interaction
architecture may underlie adverse outcomes. This observation motivates
subsequent testing of the association between hub-based spatial features
and clinical endpoints such as PFS and OS.

ecture may underlie adverse outcomes. This observation motivates
subsequent testing of the association between hub-based spatial features
and clinical endpoints such as PFS and OS. We verified that HRP tumors
with the fewest immune interaction events had the poorest
progression-free outcomes, suggesting that loss of coordinated
lymphoid--myeloid proximity may underlie immune dysfunction in these
tumors.

Collectively, these results identify immune hubs as emergent spatial
units of immune cooperation, linking genomic subtype and clinical
outcome through differences in local immune coordination. We next
investigated whether such spatial heterogeneity is further shaped by
underlying genomic instability.

## [Multi-scale heterogeneity links genomic feature with spatial TME diversity]{.mark}

analysis + WES + bulk RNA-seq?)

Whole-exome profiling identified recurrent copy-number amplifications
affecting CCNE (n = 12), MYC (n = 18), and MECOM (n = 7). These genes
were selected for further analysis owing to their known oncogenic roles
and distribution across distinct chromosomes. Ongoing refinement using
GISTIC2 peak calling and COSMIC annotation will improve the resolution
of amplification hotspots, and integration with normalized RNA-seq
expression data will verify that amplified loci correspond to high gene
expression levels.

Integrating spatial features and CNA labels revealed that copy number
alterations (CNAs) correspond to distinct spatial architectures within
the HGSC microenvironment. To further stratify the HRP cohort, we

s and CNA labels revealed that copy number
alterations (CNAs) correspond to distinct spatial architectures within
the HGSC microenvironment. To further stratify the HRP cohort, we
compared CCNE1-amplified and CCNE1-non-amplified tumors and revealed
opposite trends in spatial features, both immune infiltration and immune
proximity to TSI. In general, HRP samples with amplifications tended to
have less immune infiltration and lower immune proximity to TSI,
consistent with a more disorganized or immune-excluded microenvironment.
Noticeably, MYC and MECOM mainly affect immune density or immune
infiltration, while CCNE1 also affects the immune distribution and
distance to the TSI.

These results point to a link between genomic instability and spatial
disorganization. We will perform formal correlation analyses once CNA
validation and integration with RNA-seq expression data are complete.

Together, these observations support a model in which genomic
instability promotes spatial chaos within the TME, disrupting immune
coordination and shaping differential clinical trajectories. To
functionally contextualize these spatial and genomic patterns, we next
characterized the transcriptional programs underpinning distinct immune
and stromal niches using spatial transcriptomics.

This section provides the functional/mechanistic link between spatial
patterns and immune escape programs.

**\[Expected results example:\]** To resolve gene expression patterns
within localized tumor regions, we selected representative regions of
interest (ROIs) guided by t-CyCIF-derived spatial features and performed
spatial transcriptomics. We identified functional niches characterized
by coordinated expression of immune activation, stress response, and
stromal remodeling programs. For instance, TLS-like structures in HRP
tumors showed upregulation of B cell and chemokine signatures, while
interface regions in HRD tumors were enriched for cytotoxic and
interferon response genes. These findings illuminate the functional
specialization of spatial niches and their differential activation
across clinical subtypes.

**Integrated TME archetypes and clinical outcome**

## (TBD) iPDC validation - Independent validation supports HRD/HRP -specific immune escape mechanisms

**Other validations based on public data?**

# Methods

### Cohorts

Tumor samples and clinical data were collected in Oncosys-OVA
[[NCT06117384]{.underline}](https://clinicaltrials.gov/show/NCT06117384).
The patients' clinical details are summarized in **(Table X)**.

linical data were collected in Oncosys-OVA
[[NCT06117384]{.underline}](https://clinicaltrials.gov/show/NCT06117384).
The patients' clinical details are summarized in **(Table X)**. Patients
who don\'t receive Carboplatin in therapy and have early stage tumors
(IIB, IC1) were removed from the study. Total of 80 patients were kept
after filtering. Progression-free survival (PFS) was calculated from the
last day of observing disease relapse. Patients with PFS \> 12 (To be
decided) months were considered as long survival and categorized as
patients with long PFS, while patients with a replase in less than 12
monthes were categorized as short PFS. Patients that have been followed
for less than 365 days and did not have a relapse were not labeled with
\"long\" or \"short\" PFS, as we considered the time length is not
enough. Tissue origins include omentum, ovary, peritoneum, Adnexa and
mesentery. During analysis, \"ovary\" was considered as an early stage
tumor, \"omentum\" was considered as metastasis tumor, and the rest of
tissue origins were merged into \"mediate\" stage.

Samples for WES (n=69), bulk RNA-seq (n=69), scRNA-seq (n=76), t-CycIF
(n=90) and spatial transcriptomic **(Figure X)**, were collected from
the selected patients participating in the prospective ONCOCYS-OVA study
approved by the Ethics Committee of the Helsinki University Hospital
(HUS334/2021).

Helsinki, every patient from ONCOSYS-Ova trials provided an informed
written consent to the collection, storage, and analysis of the samples
and subsequent data.

### **t-CyCIF imaging and image processing**

The 90 samples were stained with the validated antibodies **(Table X)**
of a 17-marker panel, followed by scanning with the RareCyte CyteFinder
scanner with the t-CyCIF protocol. We used the BaSiC algorithm to
perform illumination correction, in order to remove the background
noise. ASHLAR was used to register and stitch the tiles. The raw
ome-tiff images were then segmented into single cells with StarDist with
the pre-trained model for immunofluorescence images. Each cell's mean

gister and stitch the tiles. The raw
ome-tiff images were then segmented into single cells with StarDist with
the pre-trained model for immunofluorescence images. Each cell's mean
fluorescence intensity was computed by Python package scikit-image using
in-house scripts
([[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)),
to get the single-cell marker expression table. Quality control was
performed with CyLinter. We removed cells that are in adipose tissue or
necrotic regions, and those affected by experimental artefacts, wrong
segmentation (too big or too small size) and tissue movement across
cycles. In total, 71,206,040 cells were segmented from 90 whole slide
images. After QC, 67,711,817 cells were kept. From which 3,494,223 cells
(5%) were removed during the QC step.

### t-CyCIF cell phenotyping

Log transformation was applied to the quantified single-cell signals.
Tribus was first used to assign cells semi-automatically and iteratively
into cell types using SOM-based clustering with single-cell feature
quantification table and cell phenotype marker positivity and negativity
as inputs. The logic table used for cell type characterization is shown
in **(Supplementary table X)** . Quality control for the cell type
calling was performed using the in-house Napari widget by overlaying the

for cell type characterization is shown
in **(Supplementary table X)** . Quality control for the cell type
calling was performed using the in-house Napari widget by overlaying the
cell type calls on top of the images. For problematic images, SCIMAP was
used to perform manual gating. The gate threshold files and the
phenotyping workflow file are stored in **(Supplementary table X)**.
Manual correction with in-house scripts
([[https://github.com/farkkilab/SPACE/tree/main/2_phenotyping]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/SPACE/tree/main/2_phenotyping))
were used to quantify failed rare cell type (e.g. NK cell) or serve
folder tissue, which has very uneven staining patterns. After all these
steps, 58,561,942 (86.5%) cells across 79 images were successfully
quantified with 10 cell types after the cell phenotyping workflow and
were kept for the following analysis.

### Recurrent cellular neighborhood analysis

Recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) were computed using the SCIMAP
package in Python, by first calculating the fractions of neighboring
cell types for each centering cell using \_tl.spatial_count\_ with a
r=300px (1px = 0.325µm), and the resulting neighborhood matrix was then
clustered using \_tl.spatial_cluster\_ using kmeans clustering with
k=20. 300 pixel radius was chosen to find large scale cellular

, and the resulting neighborhood matrix was then
clustered using \_tl.spatial_cluster\_ using kmeans clustering with
k=20. 300 pixel radius was chosen to find large scale cellular
structures from the spatial data, as the radius encompasses around 10-12
cells in length. The identity of each cluster was defined by the
proportions of cell types composing it.

### RCNs enrichment analysis

### The scale of cell-cell distance

We checked the distribution of the cell-cell distance scale with
SPACEstat. To characterize spatial relationships between cell types, we
computed nearest neighbor distance (NND) distributions for all pairwise
combinations of cell types across each image. For a given cell type A,
we identified the shortest Euclidean distance from each cell of type A
to the nearest cell of type B. These distances were calculated in both
directions (A to B and B to A), and the results were combined to
represent the pairwise distribution. When analyzing a single cell type
(e.g., A to A), the distance to the second-closest neighbor was used to
avoid self-matching. All distances exceeding a user-defined threshold
(if specified) were excluded.

Letting $d$ represent the Euclidean distance between cell centroids, the
nearest neighbor distance is defined as:

\$d = \\min_j \\\| \\mathbf{x}\_i - \\mathbf{x}\_j \\\|,\$ where

Letting $d$ represent the Euclidean distance between cell centroids, the
nearest neighbor distance is defined as:

\$d = \\min_j \\\| \\mathbf{x}\_i - \\mathbf{x}\_j \\\|,\$ where
\$\\mathbf{x}\_i\$ and \$\\mathbf{x}\_j\$ are the coordinates of cells
from the respective types. The distributions were computed separately
for each image and then aggregated.

### Immune-TSI study

Immune cells were spatially classified as tumor-resident or
stroma-resident based on their local microenvironment. For each immune
cell, we identified all neighboring cells within a radius of certain
value. If \>50% of these neighboring cells were annotated as tumor
cells, the immune cell was considered tumor-resident; otherwise, it was
classified as stroma-resident. This analysis was performed separately
for each sample to preserve spatial heterogeneity. Cells without any
neighbors within the radius were labeled as "undefined". The radius is
set as 80µm by checking the peak of distance between cancer and immune
cells.

### Immune network analysis

For a set of points \$P = \\{p_1, p_2, \..., p_n\\}\$ in a plane, the
Delaunay triangulation \$DT(P)\$ creates a triangulation such that no
point in \$P\$ is inside the circumcircle of any triangle in \$DT(P)\$.
For each network, the function finds connected components:

\* \$CC(L) = \\{L_1, L_2, \..., L_p\\}\$ where each \$L_i\$ is a
connected subgraph

rcle of any triangle in \$DT(P)\$.
For each network, the function finds connected components:

\* \$CC(L) = \\{L_1, L_2, \..., L_p\\}\$ where each \$L_i\$ is a
connected subgraph

\* \$CC(M) = \\{M_1, M_2, \..., M_q\\}\$ where each \$M_j\$ is a
connected subgraph

For each component, the function calculates its centroid. For example,
in lymphoid component \$L_i\$ with cells at positions \$\\{(x_1, y_1),
(x_2, y_2), \..., (x_n, y_n)\\}\$: \$C\_{L_i} =
(\\frac{1}{n}\\sum\_{j=1}\^{n}x_j, \\frac{1}{n}\\sum\_{j=1}\^{n}y_j)\$.

A bipartite graph \$G\_{proximity}\$ is constructed where:

\* Nodes represent lymphoid and myeloid components

\* Edges connect components whose centroids are within the proximity
threshold

For centroids \$C\_{L_i}\$ and \$C\_{M_j}\$, an edge exists if:
\$\\\|C\_{L_i} - C\_{M_j}\\\| \\leq \\text{proximity/threshold}\$, where
\$\\\|C\_{L_i} - C\_{M_j}\\\|\$ is the Euclidean distance:
\$\\\|C\_{L_i} - C\_{M_j}\\\| = \\sqrt{(C\_{L_i,x} - C\_{M_j,x})\^2 +
(C\_{L_i,y} - C\_{M_j,y})\^2}\$.

Immune hubs are identified as connected components in
\$G\_{proximity}\$: \$\\text{Hubs} = CC(G\_{proximity})\$. Each hub
contains at least one lymphoid component and one myeloid component.

For each hub \$H\$:

\* Hub size is calculated as \$\\text{size}(H) = \\sum\_{v \\in H}
\\text{size}(v)\$.

\* Lymphoid-myeloid ratio: \$\\text{ratio}(H) = \\frac{\\sum\_{v \\in H,

or each hub \$H\$:

\* Hub size is calculated as \$\\text{size}(H) = \\sum\_{v \\in H}
\\text{size}(v)\$.

\* Lymphoid-myeloid ratio: \$\\text{ratio}(H) = \\frac{\\sum\_{v \\in H,
\\text{type}(v)=\\text{lymphoid}} \\text{size}(v)}{\\max(1, \\sum\_{v
\\in H, \\text{type}(v)=\\text{myeloid}} \\text{size}(v))}\$.

If min_hub_size is specified, only hubs where \$\\text{size}(H) \\geq
\\text{min hub size}\$ are retained.

### Clinical analysis - for Venla 

Cox model etc.

Survival analyses were performed using the R packages survival and
survminer. We modeled overall survival by using Kaplan-Meier estimates
from survfit with 95% confidence intervals and log-rank p-values from
ggsurvplot. For the Cox proportional hazard model, we used the variables
that were selected in the variable selection pipeline. The results were
visualized using forest plots with 95% confidence intervals. Results
were considered statistically significant in all analyses, when p-values
were less than 0.05.

### Feature selections - Venla

Correlation analysis between categorical variables was done by using
Cramer's V. This provides standardized measures of association in the
range of 0 (no association) to 1 (perfect association). Statistical
significance was calculated using Pearson's chi-square tests with
significance level α = 0.05.

For continuous variables we created a pipeline, which includes Variable

Statistical
significance was calculated using Pearson's chi-square tests with
significance level α = 0.05.

For continuous variables we created a pipeline, which includes Variable
Inflation Factor (VIF), LASSO and Random Forest to check the
multicollinearity, reduce dimensionality and select the most predictive
features.

The VIF threshold is selected to be 5, so any variable that gives high
multicollinearity (VIF \> 5) is not removed. LASSO shrinks some
coefficients to zero removing least important variables. LASSO uses
cv.glmnet for cross-validation for the optimal lambda. Random Forest
builds 1000 survival trees using the randomForestSRC package. The
variables with importance score \> 0 were selected from Random Forest.
We combined the three variable selection methods and selected the
variables that were selected at least in two of the three variable
selection methods.

Before adding these selected variables into the Cox model, we performed
final feature selection, using recursive feature elimination. We used a
repeated cross-validation C-index function and selected the feature
combination to the Cox model, that gave the best model performance.

### Whole Exome sequencing analysis & iMTB reports - for Andreas

### Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification

Copy-number profiles were derived from segmented sequencing data using

cing analysis & iMTB reports - for Andreas

### Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification

Copy-number profiles were derived from segmented sequencing data using
ASCAT, which provides allele-specific copy-number estimates together
with tumour purity and ploidy inference (Van Loo et al., 2010). Given
the high degree of aneuploidy in HGSC, copy-number alterations were
defined using ploidy-normalised values, calculated as:

$LogR\ = Log_{2}\frac{CNtotal}{ploidy}\ $

where *CNtotal* denotes the ASCAT-derived total copy number ( nAraw +
nBraw), and *ploidy* corresponds to the tumour ploidy estimate for the
respective sample. Segments with logR values greater than 1.3 were
classified as amplifications, whereas segments with values below −1 were
classified as deletions. The amplification threshold approximately
corresponds to copy-number states exceeding \~2.5-fold above tumour
ploidy and was selected to enrich for high-level focal amplification
events. Conversely, the deletion threshold corresponds approximately to
copy-number states below half the tumour ploidy and was intended to
capture substantial allelic loss and deep deletion events. Segments that
did not meet these criteria were considered copy-number neutral in
downstream analyses.

Fraction of Genome Altered (FGA) was calculated as the proportion of the
evaluated genome affected by copy-number alterations:

sidered copy-number neutral in
downstream analyses.

Fraction of Genome Altered (FGA) was calculated as the proportion of the
evaluated genome affected by copy-number alterations:

$FGA\ = \ \frac{\Sigma\ L_{altered}}{L_{genome}}$

where *Laltered* represents the cumulative genomic length of segments
classified as amplified or deleted, and *Lgenome* denotes the total
genomic length assessed. Only segments classified as amplified or
deleted according to the thresholds defined above were included in the
altered fraction. For downstream analyses, samples were ranked according
to FGA values and stratified into tertiles representing low,
intermediate, and high levels of genomic alteration burden.

Whole-genome doubling (WGD) was inferred from allele-specific
copy-number profiles using the framework described by Zhang et al.
(2025), based on the fraction of the genome with major allele copy
number ≥2 (FM2). For each sample, the fraction was calculated as:

$FM2\ = \ \frac{\Sigma\ L_{major\ CN\ > 2}}{L_{genome}}$

where *Lmajor CN≥2* denotes the cumulative genomic length of segments
with major-allele copy number ≥2. Samples with FM2 values exceeding 0.5
were classified as WGD-positive according to the original framework. FM2
values were clustered using k-means (*k* = 3) and ordered by increasing
genome-doubling burden (WGD0--WGD2).
